---
title: "Cognitive Biases & Heuristics"
author: "Anissa Chan"
format:
  revealjs:
    width: 1000
    height: 562
    margin: 0
    center: false
    transition: none
    slide-number: false
    progress: false
    controls: true
    css: styles.css
---

<div class="slide-frame title-slide">
  <div class="top-gradient"></div>
  <div class="main-title">Cognitive Biases &amp; Heuristics</div>
  <div class="line"></div>
  <div class="author">Anissa<br>Chan</div>
</div>

::: {.notes}
Everyone has some sort of bias. Whether that be preferring cheeseburgers over hotdogs or fantasy over sci-fi. It affects everything: who we hangout with, what we believe, how we interact with the world. Data is no different even when it seems like it is. That’s why in this presentation I would like to inform you about the different types of cognitive biases and heuristics, particularly the anchoring effect, confirmation bias, and the availability heuristic.
:::

---

<div class="slide-frame">
  <img class="contain-img" src="assets/anchoring-cars.png" alt="Anchoring effect car pricing example" style="left: 3px; top: 50px; width: 506px; height: 513px;">

  <div class="slide-title" style="left: 509px; top: 90px; width: 449px;">Anchoring Effect</div>

  <div class="body-copy" style="left: 519px; top: 158px; width: 449px;">
    <span class="label">What is it?</span>
    <ul>
      <li>Overreliance on first information seen.</li>
    </ul>
    <div style="height: 18px;"></div>
    <span class="label">Seen in Data Analysis</span>
    <ul>
      <li>Updated information is neglected</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

::: {.notes}
Let’s first talk about the Anchoring Effect. According to Harvard, “the anchoring effect is a cognitive bias…[of] relying too heavily on the first piece of information offered”. We see an example of this in the picture to the left. On the top, the customer sees the price tag of 30k for this new car, which he remarks as too expensive. However, what if he had seen the 60k price tag first and then saw the second price tag of 30k. Well, relatively the 30k seems like a good deal comparatively, right? That is what the Anchoring Effect is. We see this in Data Analytics where newer information can become neglected as we lean onto previous insights.
:::

---

<div class="slide-frame">
  <div class="slide-title" style="left: 51px; top: 105px; width: 449px;">Confirmation Bias</div>

  <div class="body-copy" style="left: 68px; top: 166px; width: 351px;">
    <span class="label">What is it?</span>
    <ul>
      <li>Tendency to ignore information that does not affirm preset beliefs</li>
    </ul>
    <div style="height: 18px;"></div>
    <span class="label">Seen in Data Analysis</span>
    <ul>
      <li>Experiments are ran to confirm beliefs</li>
      <li>New ideas passed over</li>
    </ul>
  </div>

  <img class="contain-img" src="assets/confirmation-venn.png" alt="Confirmation bias diagram" style="left: 450px; top: 67px; width: 518px; height: 428px;">
  <img class="contain-img fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index="1" src="assets/confirmation-blueberries.jpeg" alt="Blueberries photo" style="left: 437px; top: 16px; width: 542px; height: 362px;">
  <img class="contain-img fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index="2" src="assets/confirmation-typing.gif" alt="Typing GIF" style="left: 450px; top: 242px; width: 518px; height: 311px;">
</div>

::: {.notes}
Next, we will talk about a confirmation bias. A confirmation bias is, according to the Oxford Review, “the tendency of individuals to seek, interpret, and remember information in a way that confirms their pre-existing beliefs or values”. What this leads to is ignoring information that doesn’t coincide with other beliefs. Let me give you an example from my own personal life. As someone who tries to eat healthy and grocery shops for themselves, I tend to buy many perishables. However, many perishables like fruit and vegetables don’t have an expiration date written on them. Many times, I come across the situation where a product say blueberries that I bought only four day ago have started to grow some mold, which probably means they’re coming up at time they should be thrown out. Wait, doesn’t that mean I should be eating them soon? but I really really don’t want to and they’re supposed to last longer that. So I’ll search up on Google “how long do blueberries last in the fridge?”. I’ll be scrolling and scrolling until I find range of days that satisfies me. That is what confirmation bias is. We see this in data analysis and experimentation where we tend to run experiment or in fact, interpret the information from experiment as something that already confirms our beliefs, hindering our critical thinking about what truly could be the driver of causality as an example. The data collected from this as well could have created newer, interesting ideas which are then ignored due to this bias.
:::

---

<div class="slide-frame">
  <img class="contain-img" src="assets/availability-cartoon.png" alt="Availability heuristic illustration" style="left: 38px; top: 133px; width: 445px; height: 297px;">
  <img class="contain-img fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index="1" src="assets/availability-table.png" alt="Shark attack table" style="left: 32px; top: 101px; width: 455px; height: 361px;">

  <div class="slide-title" style="left: 509px; top: 90px; width: 449px;">Availability heuristic</div>

  <div class="body-copy" style="left: 519px; top: 158px; width: 449px;">
    <span class="label">What is it?</span>
    <ul>
      <li>Tendency to rely on memorable ideas and events</li>
    </ul>
    <div style="height: 18px;"></div>
    <span class="label">Seen in Data Analysis</span>
    <ul>
      <li>Overemphasize information that is more memorable</li>
      <li>Ignoring information that is less memorable</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

::: {.notes}
Finally, we will talk about the availability heuristic. The availability heuristic focuses on how we as humans tend to recall upon ideas and events that more memorable, usually in situations in which the information is surprising, shocking, or bad. It’s why many people fear the ocean as they are afraid that a shark might bite them like seen in movies like Jaws and new reports of shark attacks. Even though the International Shark Attack File of 2025 reported that worldwide there were only 65 unprovoked shark bites on humans and 29 provoked bites. In the US, there were only 26 total shark bites with a majority of them being in Florida. Now, how does this relate to data analysis. Well, in terms of data, we will tend to focus on the information that carries more impact while pushing to the side information that isn’t even when the information found can be relevant.
:::

---

<div class="slide-frame">
  <div class="center-title" style="top: 182px;">Conclusion</div>
  <img class="bottom-photo" src="assets/bottom-photo.jpg" alt="Desk photo">
</div>

::: {.notes}
In conclusion, biases and heuristics are part of our everyday way of thinking. As analyst and experimenters, it is our duty to catch ourselves on these, using techniques like critical thinking and taking outliers into account, and create ways to not let them affect our practices like through randomization and blind testing. I hope that knowing more about these effects will help you all in your analytics journey. Thank you!
:::

---

<div class="slide-frame">
  <div class="sources-title">Sources</div>
  <div class="sources-list small-links">
    <div><a href="https://www.floridamuseum.ufl.edu/shark-attacks/yearly-worldwide-summary/">https://www.floridamuseum.ufl.edu/shark-attacks/yearly-worldwide-summary/</a></div>
    <div><a href="https://www.pon.harvard.edu/tag/anchoring-effect/">https://www.pon.harvard.edu/tag/anchoring-effect/</a></div>
    <div><a href="https://lpsonline.sas.upenn.edu/features/cognitive-bias-and-data-how-human-psychology-impacts-data-interpretation">https://lpsonline.sas.upenn.edu/features/cognitive-bias-and-data-how-human-psychology-impacts-data-interpretation</a></div>
    <div><a href="https://oxford-review.com/the-oxford-review-dei-diversity-equity-and-inclusion-dictionary/confirmation-bias-definition-and-explanation/">https://oxford-review.com/the-oxford-review-dei-diversity-equity-and-inclusion-dictionary/confirmation-bias-definition-and-explanation/</a></div>
  </div>
  <img class="bottom-photo" src="assets/bottom-photo.jpg" alt="Desk photo">
</div>
